question: If stationery bought for N200 has been entered as N2000. To correct this error?

To correct the error, we have to debit the amount in excess so as when it is being calculated, the debit side would balance with the credit side.

The business owner made a provision of 500 as bad debt. Total debt owed was 20,000. At the end of the period, bad debts was 4%. Hence we have; 

4% x 20000
0.04 x 20000 = 800

we subtract the total bad debt from the amount that was initially provided for bad debt and charge it to the profit and loss account as a expense

question: Calculate the cash balance after the discount
solution:
cash balance = debit side of the cash column - credit side of cash column. Hence we have;

Discount allowed = 0.02 x 2000 = 40

Debit column = 3000 + 1000 + 2000 + 200 = 6200 
6200 - 40 (cash discount) = 6160

credit column = 200 + 3000 + 200 = 3400

6160 - 3400 = 2760

solution:
Turnover ratio is the cost of goods sold divided by the average stock for the same period

Cost of goods sold = opening stock + purchases - closing stock
25,120 + 44,880 - 27,840 = 42,160

Average stock = opening stock + closing stock / 2
25,120 + 27,840 = 52960 / 2 = 26,480

Turn over = 42,160 / 26,480 = 1.59 times Approximately 2 times

question: The following accounting entries are made when bad debt is recovered
solution:
At times a debtor whose account had earlier been written off by a creditor as a bad debt may decide to make a payment, this is called recovery of bad debts. While posting the journal entry for recovery of bad debts it is important to note that it is treated as a gain to the business & that the debtor should not be credited as in case of sales.

While journalizing for bad debts, debtor’s personal account is credited and bad debts account is debited because bad debts written off are treated as a loss to the business and now when they are recovered it is seen as a fresh gain.

Journal entry for recovery of bad debts is as follows;

Cash or Bank A/C Debit Real A/C Dr. What comes in
 To Bad Debts Recovered A/C Credit Nominal A/C Cr. income & gains

Debit (Cash or Bank) depending on how the money is received

Rules applied as per modern or US style of accounting 

Cash/Bank A/c Debit the increase in assets
Bad Debts Recovered A/c Credit the increase in income

The closing journal entry for bad debts recovered would be as follows;

Bad Debts Recovered A/C  Debit
 To Profit and Loss A/C  Credit

options.a: debit bad debts accounts and credit bad debt recovered account
options.b: debit debtors accounts and recovered account
options.c: debit bad debts recovered account and credit bad debts account
options.d: debit bad debts account and credit profit and loss account

The major advantage of the first in first out(FIFO) method of stock valuation

question: Ade, Tony, Rose share profit and losses in the ratio of 3 : 2 : 1 respectively. Ade retires and the remaining partners decide to take Ade’s share in the existing ratio. What is the new ratio?
solution:
The existing ratio between Tony and Rose = 2/6 and 1/6

  Ade’s ratio = 3/6

  Ade’s share to be taken by Tony and Rose in the ratio 2 : 1

  Tony gets: 3/6 x 2/6

  = 6/18

  Rose gets: 3/6 x 1/3

  = 3/18

  New ratio between Tony and Rose = 6 : 3

  = 2 : 1

question: The control account is used in facilitating?
solution:
A control account, often called a controlling account, is a general ledger account that summarizes and combines all of the subsidiary accounts for a specific type. In other words, it’s a summary account that equals the sum of the subsidiary account and is used to simplify and organize the general ledger. The purpose of the control account is to keep the general ledger nice and clean without any details, yet contain the correct balances to be used in the financial statements. 

  • Control accounts allow a single trial balance to be extracted from the general ledger.
  • If the trial balance does not balance, only the accounts whose control account does not reconcile need to be checked for errors.
  • A different person can maintain the control account as a check against fraud.
  • Control accounts speed up the process of producing management accounts information as the control account balance can be used without waiting for the individual balances to be reconciled and extracted.
  • Control accounts reduce the amount of detail needed in the general ledger.

question: The main difference between the ordinary and preference shareholders is that?
solution:
An ordinary share defines a single unit of equity ownership of a corporation, where the holders of the ordinary shares receive the right to cast a vote in decisions involving important corporate matters. Such votes are available to each ordinary shareholder in correspondence to the number of ordinary shares held within the company. Ordinary shareholders are the last to receive dividends, and are only entitled to funds which remain after dividends on preferred shares are paid. Ordinary share holders may not receive dividend payments every year, and payments to ordinary shareholders depend on reinvestment decisions made by the company directors. In an event of the company facing liquidation, the ordinary shareholders will be the last to receive their share of funds, after the creditors and preference shareholders are paid. As such ordinary shares are riskier than bonds or preference shares. Ordinary shares are also referred to as ‘common stock’.

Preference shares are offered preference in relation to ordinary shares, where the preference shareholder receives dividends before ordinary shareholders are paid out. Preference shareholders are paid a fixed dividend and have the first claim on the assets and earnings. As such, preference shareholders receive their share of the firm’s residual value before ordinary shareholders in the event of liquidation. Preference shareholders do not have voting rights.

question: Bala ltd acquired the business of bello ltd and caused the separate existence of the latter company to terminate.
solution:
A merger is an agreement that unites two existing companies into one new company. There are several types of mergers and also several reasons why companies complete mergers. Mergers and acquisitions are commonly done to expand a company’s reach, expand into new segments, or gain market share. All of these are done to please shareholders and create value.

A merger is the voluntary fusion of two companies on broadly equal terms into one new legal entity. The firms that agree to merge are roughly equal in terms of size, customers, scale of operations, etc. For this reason, the term "merger of equals" is sometimes used. Acquisitions, unlike mergers, or generally not voluntary and involve one company actively purchasing another.

Mergers are most commonly done to gain market share, reduce costs of operations, expand to new territories, unite common products, grow revenues, and increase profits—all of which should benefit the firms' shareholders. After a merger, shares of the new company are distributed to existing shareholders of both original businesses.

Due to a large number of mergers, a mutual fund emerged, giving investors a chance to profit from merger deals. The fund captures the spread or amount left between the offer price and trading price. The Merger Fund from Westchester Capital Funds has been around since 1989. The fund invests in companies that have publicly announced a merger or takeover. To invest in the fund, a minimum amount of $2,000 is required, with a 1.91% expense ratio. As of March 2, 2019, the fund has returned 6.1% annually since inception in 1989.

question: In order to make the cash book balance equal to the bank statement, it is usually to add?
solution:
we will look at the way in which a business deals with any differences between the balance of the bank account in the cash book and the closing balance of the bank account shown by the bank statement for the same period.
These differences are explained by a document known as a bank reconciliation statement: which lists
• items which are in the cash book but not on the bank statement
• items which are on the bank statement but not in the cash book
This process is an important one: it enables the business to update its cash book and also helps to prove the accuracy of the bookkeeping of the business and the bank.

 1 Prepare a Bank Reconciliation Statement
(a) Compare transactions that appear on both Cash Book and Bank Statement
(b) Update Cash Book from details of transactions appearing on Bank Statement
(c)  Balance the bank columns of the Cash Book to calculate the revised balance

2 Complete a Bank Reconciliation Statement
(a) Enter correct date of the statement
(b) Enter the balance at bank as per the Cash Book

question: Kakaku limited with three departments has a total of N7,200,000 as net debtors for the year ended 31/12/2106. The company's policy, provides for 15%bad debt annually. Which of the following represents the total balance of debtors before adjustment?
solution:
What is 'Net Debt'?. Net debt shows a business's overall financial situation by subtracting the total value of its cash, cash equivalents, and other liquid assets, a process called netting. All the information necessary to determine a company's net debt can be found on its balance sheet.

A bad debt is a monetary amount owed to a creditor that is unlikely to be paid and, or which the creditor is not willing to take action to collect for various reasons, often due to the debtor not having the money to pay, for example due to a company going into liquidation or insolvency.

net debtors = 7,200,000 (after all necessary deductions have been made)

bad debts = 15% ( an amount to be deducted from the total of all the debts owed to the company)

The question says, ''the total balance of debtors before adjustment'' Remember net debtors is gotten after necessary deductions are made. This means that, the 15% provided as bad debts to be written off by the company, had already been deducted as well. to get the initial balance before the deductions, we simply get 15% of 7,200,000 and add it to the net debtors. Hence we have;

15/100 * 7,200,000 = N1,080,000

7,200,000 + 1,080,000 = N8,280,000

A machine bought for ₦35,000 was estimated to have a life span of 5 years with a scrap value of ₦9000.
 

The yearly depreciation using the straight-line method would be
solution:
To calculate the yearly depreciation using the straight-line method, use the formula;

Yearly Depreciation = \(\frac{\text{cost of asset - scrap value}}{\text{useful life}}\)

=  \(\frac{35,000 - 9,000}{5}\) = \(\frac{26,000}{5}\) = #5200.

question:
Dairo and Segun are in partnership sharing profits and losses in the ratio 2:3 respectively. The information below relates to their business for the year ended 31st December, 2018.

         ₦
                   Dairo        12000
                   Segun        18000
                   Capital:   
                   Dairo        120000
                   Segun         60000

- Interest on drawings 10%

- Interest on capital 5%

- Profit for the year ₦36,000

- Salary: Segun ₦10,000
Segun's share of profit is
solution:
Interest on drawings = 10%
- Dairo = 10% x 12,0000
= ₦1200
- Segun = 10% x 18,000
= ₦1800

Interest on capital = 5%
- Dairo = 5% x 120,000
= ₦6,000
- Segun = 5% x 60,000
=₦3,000

Salary: segun = ₦10,000

Segun's share of profit = (Net profit + int on drawings) - ( int on capital + segun salary )
= (36000 + 1200 + 1800) - ( 6000 + 3000 + 10000)
= 39000 - 19000
= 20,000

Total ratio = 2+ 3
= 5
Therefore, Segun share of profit = 3 /5 x 20000
= ₦12,000

solution:

Credit: Supplier (#6,000)

Debit: A debit increases an asset account. In this case, "Machinery" is an asset account that represents the new equipment Tolu acquired.

Credit: A credit increases a liability account. Since Tolu purchases the machine on credit, they now owe money

question: An equipment costing #9,000 has an estimated residual value of #900, and is depreciated at 10% per annum. Using the straight-line method, what is the depreciation charge for the second year?
solution:
To calculate depreciation using the straight-line method, you can use the formula:
Depreciation Expense = (Cost of Asset - Residual Value) / Useful Life
Given:
Cost of the equipment = 9,000 Residual value = 900
Depreciation rate = 10%
Useful life = 10 years (since the depreciation rate is 10%)
First, calculate the annual depreciation:
Depreciation Expense = (9,000 - 900) / 10
= 8,100 / 10
= 810
Therefore, the depreciation charge for the second year would still be #810.
subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts

question: An equipment costing # 9,000 has an estimated residual value of #900, and is depreciated at 10% per annum. What is the depreciation charge for the three using diminishing balance method?
solution:
Net Book Value at the beginning of Year 2 = Original cost - Depreciation charge for Year 1
Year 1 depreciation charge = Original cost x Depreciation rate
= #9,000 x 10%
= #900
Net Book Value at the beginning of Year 2 = #9,000 - #900
= #8,100
Now, we can calculate the depreciation charge for Year 2 using the diminishing balance method:
Depreciation charge for Year 2 = Net Book Value at the beginning of Year 2 x Depreciation rate = # 8,100 x 10%
= #810
Finally, we can calculate the depreciation charge for Year 3 using the same method:
Net Book Value at the beginning of Year 3 = Net Book Value at the beginning of Year 2 - Depreciation charge for Year 2
= #8,100 - #810
= #7,290
Depreciation charge for Year 3 = Net Book Value at the beginning of Year 3 x Depreciation rate
= #7,290 x 10%
= #729

question: Ordinary shares are also known as
solution:
Ordinary shares, also known as common shares or equity shares, represent ownership in a company. When individuals or investors purchase ordinary shares, they become shareholders and have ownership rights in the company.
Equity refers to the ownership interest or residual claim on the assets of a company after deducting liabilities. Ordinary shares represent the equity portion of a company's capital structure, and shareholders who hold ordinary shares have voting rights and the potential to receive dividends.
subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts

question: Which of the following is a function of the operating system of a computer?
solution:
The operating system acts as a bridge between the hardware (physical components) and the software (programs
on a computer. It manages various tasks, including:
Controlling peripheral devices (printers, scanners, etc.)
Managing memory allocation
Handling file systems
Providing a user interface (UI) for interaction
Facilitating process execution (running programs)

options.a: controls the input and output peripherals

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts

question: The value of assets on dissolution of partnership is debited to realization account and credited to ___ account
solution:
The realization account is used to record the sale of assets and settlement of liabilities during the dissolution process.
When assets are sold, cash is received. This cash is credited to the cash account to reflect the increase in cash holdings.
Ultimately, the goal of dissolution is to convert all assets into cash, settle liabilities, and distribute any remainin cash among the partners according to their profit-sharing ratios.
subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts

question:
The following extracts are made from the books of Agama Enterprises.

Motor van (cost)

 120000

Life span

 4 years

rate of Depreciation

 40%

Method of depreciation used is Diminishing Balance The scrap value of the asset at the end of year four is
solution:
Year 1:

Depreciation = (Book value at the beginning of the year) x (Depreciation rate) = (#120,000) x (40%)
= #48,000
Book value at the end of Year 1 = Cost - Depreciation
= #120,000 - #48,000
= #72,000
Year 2:
Depreciation = (Book value at the beginning of the year) x (Depreciation rate) = (#72,000) x (40%)
= #28,800
Book value at the end of Year 2 = Book value at the end of Year 1 - Depreciation = #72,000 - #28,800
= #43,200
Year 3:
Depreciation = (Book value at the beginning of the year) x (Depreciation rate) = (#43,200) x (40%)
= #17,280
Book value at the end of Year 3 = Book value at the end of Year 2 - Depreciation = #43,200 - #17,280
= #25,920
Year 4:
Depreciation = (Book value at the beginning of the vear) x (Depreciation rate)
subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts

question:

Motor van (cost)

 120000

Life span

 4 years

rate of Depreciation

 40%

Method of depreciation used is Diminishing Balance The depreciation charge for year two is
solution:
First, we calculate the depreciation for the first year: Depreciation for year one = Cost x Rate of Depreciation = 120,000 x 40%
= 48,000
Next, we calculate the remaining book value after the first year: Book value after year one = Cost - Depreciation for year one
= 120,000 - 48,000
= 72,000
To calculate the depreciation charge for year two, we apply the rate of depreciation to the remaining book value Depreciation for year two = Remaining book value x Rate of Depreciation
= 72,000 x 40%
= 28,800

solution:

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts

question: A computer set bought for #150,000 was disposed for #45,000 after some years of use. The profit on disposal was #7,500. Accumulated depreciation at the time of disposal was
solution:
Accumulated depreciation
= Cost − Disposal price − Profit
= ₦150,000 − ₦45,000 − ₦7,500
= ₦97,500.
subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts

question: Which of the following terms is not used to describe the total amount stated in the memorandum of association approved by the Registrar of Companies?
solution:
The term 'issued capital' does not describe the total amount stated in the Memorandum of Association. The memorandum of association states the company’s authorized (nominal) capital, also known as registered capital, which is the maximum share capital the company is permitted to issue.

Issued capital refers only to the portion of the authorized capital that has actually been issued to shareholders and fully paid. Therefore, issued capital is not the total amount stated in the memorandum of association.
subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts

question: Shares issued below the nominal value are referred to as shares at
solution: Shares issued at a nominal value below the actual value are known as shares issued at a discount. The nominal value (par value) of a share is its face value. When shares are issued at a price lower than this face value, they are said to be issued at a discount.
subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts

question: Sulah took two textile materials worth GH¢ 500 from his business for his children's use. This would be treated as
solution: When a business owner takes goods for personal use, it is treated as drawings. Drawings are withdrawals of business assets by the owner for personal use. Since Sulah took textile materials worth GH¢500 for his children, this is treated as drawings.
subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts

question: Discounts received are
solution: Discounts received are a gain to the business and are therefore credited to the Profit and Loss Account. Since it is an income, the Discount Received account is credited. At the end of the period, it is transferred to the Profit and Loss Account by crediting the Profit and Loss Account.

options.a: credited to the Profit and Loss Account
options.d: debited to the Profit and Loss Account

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts

question: Below-the-line item in public sector accounting means such an item is
solution: A below-the-line item in public sector accounting refers to an item that is not budgeted for in the current fiscal year. Such items are not included in the main budget and usually arise from unexpected or unplanned expenses.

options.a: less than what is budgeted for
options.b: not budgeted for in the current fiscal year
options.c: to be carried forward to the next fiscal year
options.d: budgeted for in the current fiscal year

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts

question: Which of the following is not part of prime cost of production?
solution: Prime cost of production consists of direct costs such as raw materials, direct labour, and other direct expenses. Depreciation of factory equipment is an indirect cost and therefore not included in prime cost.
subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts

question: A feature of government accounting is that the
solution: Government accounting is mainly based on the cash basis of accounting. Transactions are recorded only when cash is received or paid. Unlike the accrual basis, receivables and payables are not recognized.

options.a: Accounts are prepared on an accrual basis.
options.b: Accounts are prepared on a cash basis.
options.d: objective is to report profit made by the government.

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts

question: A bank statement shows an overdraft of GH¢190,000. Kofi, a debtor, paid GH¢400,000 into the account. The new bank balance is
solution:
The account is overdrawn by GH¢190,000.
When Kofi, a debtor, pays GH¢400,000 into the account, the overdraft is cleared, and the new balance becomes GH¢210,000 positive (400,000 − 190,000 = 210,000).
subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts

question: Capital receipt is collected
solution: Capital receipt is a non-recurring income that creates a liability or reduces an asset. It is received from the sale of fixed assets, such as a motor vehicle. Sales of stock, trade debtors, or trading activities are regular business income and are classified as revenue receipts, not capital receipts.

options.b: on sales of stock in trade

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts

question: Which of the following is a component of prime cost?
solution: Prime costs are expenses that can be directly charged to a specific unit of production. Royalty is a direct expense and is included in prime cost. All other options are classified as factory overhead.
subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts

question: The accounting principle that states that, in the preparation of an accounting statement, revenue is recognized as soon as goods are passed on to the customer is the
solution: The realization concept states that revenue is recognized when it is earned and realizable, not when cash is received. This means revenue is recorded when goods are delivered or services provided. Thus, recognizing revenue as soon as goods are passed to the customer follows the realization concept.
subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts

question: In departmental accounts, rent is apportioned on the basis of
solution: In departmental accounting, expenses are apportioned to departments using a fair basis. Rent is usually apportioned based on the floor area occupied by each department, as space occupied reflects the benefit each department gains from the rent.

options.d: number of personnel in each department

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts

question: The process of recording the financial transactions of government is
solution: Public sector accounting involves recording, summarizing, analyzing, and reporting the financial transactions of the government. It covers the receipt, custody, and use of public funds, as well as accountability for them. Therefore, recording government financial transactions is part of public sector accounting.
subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts

question: A business should not lay claim to any profit before it is earned. This is in accordance with the
solution: The prudence concept means a business should not anticipate profits but must provide for all possible losses. Profits should only be recognized when they are earned.
subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts

question: Which of the following errors affects the agreement of a Trial Balance?
solution: The trial balance will not agree if there is an error involving a single entry, such as a wrong addition in the sales account. This is because the trial balance totals all debits and credits in a double-entry system. Errors affecting two accounts will not affect the trial balance agreement.

options.a: Posting the Purchase of a van to the debit side of the purchases account
options.b: Failure to enter sales in the books
options.c: Wrong addition in the sales account
options.d: Crediting a purchase to A. Tambi's account instead of F. Tambi's account

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts

question: Which of the following is an advantage of the imprest system?
solution: The imprest system is used to control petty cash. Its main advantage is efficient management of small expenses. It does not directly increase profits, reward the imprest holder, or simplify final account preparation.

options.a: making high profits in the business
options.d: Easy preparation of the final accounts

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts

question: The document which sets out the internal arrangement for the proper management of a company is the
solution: The Articles of Association set out the internal rules for managing a company, including shareholders’ rights, directors’ powers, and meeting procedures. The Memorandum of Association governs the company’s external relations. The Prospectus invites the public to subscribe to shares, while the Certificate of Incorporation proves the company’s legal existence.
subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts

question: Which of the following is the basis of accounting in public service?
solution: Public service accounting uses the cash basis, meaning revenues are recorded when cash is received and expenses when paid. This differs from the accrual basis, where transactions are recorded when earned or incurred, regardless of cash flow.
subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts

question: Which of the following is not part of the double-entry system?
solution: The balance sheet, also called the statement of financial position, shows a business’s assets, liabilities, and capital. It is a financial statement, not a part of the double-entry bookkeeping process.
subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts

question: The head office usually issues goods to branches at
solution: The head office issues goods to branches at cost price because it reflects the actual production cost without any profit. This gives the most accurate value of goods transferred to the branch.
subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts

question:
Receipts and Payments Account of Abalure Youth Club for the year ended 31st December 2018

Subscriptions for 2017 and 2019 received during the year were #180,000 and #150,000, respectively.

The surplus for the year 2018 was
solution: Surplus = 11,930,000 (i.e the addition of all income in the receipts and payment account) - 8,095,000 (from total expenses) = #5,235,000
subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts

question: In the absence of a partnership agreement, additional capital contributions by partners attract interest of
solution: If there is no partnership agreement, the Partnership Act 1890 applies. The Act states that no interest is allowed on capital. However, partners may agree to pay interest, commonly at 5% per annum, but this is not legally required.
subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts

question: According to the entity concept, ownership is
solution: The entity concept states that a business is separate from its owners. Business transactions are recorded independently of owners’ personal transactions, ensuring financial statements reflect the business’s activities only.

options.d: vested on the board of directors

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts

question: If sales is  D 12,000, and the gross profit markup percentage is 25%. What is the cost of sales?
solution:
Gross profit = 25% × D12,000 = D3,000
Cost of sales = Total sales − Gross profit = 12,000 − 3,000 = D9,000
subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts

question: A suspense account is used to
solution: A suspense account is a temporary account used to balance the trial balance when there is a discrepancy. It holds the difference until errors are found and corrected.
subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts

question: Which of the following is apportioned in proportion of the purchases of each department?
solution: Trading and profit or loss accounts are prepared for each department following normal procedures. Common expenses are apportioned, while discount received is apportioned based on each department’s purchases.
subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts

question: The transfer of goods between departments is recorded by debiting
solution: When goods are transferred between departments, the receiving department is debited (receiving an asset), and the giving department is credited (giving up an asset). This follows the double-entry principle. Therefore, the correct treatment is debiting the receiving and crediting the giving department.

options.a: stock account and crediting trading account
options.b: trading account and crediting purchases account
options.c: the receiving department and crediting the giving department
options.d: the giving department and crediting the receiving department

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts

question: The Chief Accounting Officer of the federation is
solution: The Chief Accounting Officer of the federation is the Accountant General, who supervises the receipt, custody, and payment of public funds. The Minister of Finance manages overall financial policy, while the Auditor General audits public accounts. The Chief Accountant is not a recognized federal position.
subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts

question: Which of the following is not true of a trial balance?
solution: A trial balance is an internal report used within the accounting department. It lists all debit and credit balances from the ledger for a period to check that total debits equal total credits.

options.a: list taken of the account balance in the general ledger
options.b: proof of the arithmetical accuracy of the ledger postings
options.c: list of account abstracted from a subsidiary ledger to check their agreement with the related control account
options.d: summary of data for the preparation of financial statement

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts

question:
Use the following information to answer this question

A fixed asset was bought for #60,000 on 1st January, 1997. Depreciation was provided at 10% on cost. It was sold for #16,000 on 30th June, 2001.

The profit or loss sale was
solution:
Calculation of depreciation

 = \(\frac{10}{100} \times 60, 000\) = # 6000

Depreciation at the year of disposal = \(\frac{6}{12}\) x \(\frac{10}{100}\) x 60,000 = #3000

  #    
Cost 60,000 provision for depreciation 27,000
    Cash realised from sales of assets 16,000
    loss on sales  17,000

                 60000                                                                                                60,000
subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts

question:
Use the following information to answer this question

A fixed asset was bought for #60,000 on 1st January, 1997. Depreciation was provided at 10% on cost. It was sold for #16,000 on 30th June, 2001.

The net book value at the time of sale was
solution:
Net book value at the time of sales = cost - Accumulated depreciation at the time of sales

=60,000 - 27,000

Net book value = #33,000
subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts

question: Receipts and payments account discloses
solution: Capital items involve the purchase or sale of fixed assets and other long-term investments. These transactions are recorded in the Receipts and Payments account because they involve actual cash flows.
subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts

question: In partnership dissolution, an asset taken over by a partner is debited to
solution: The accounting entries are: Debit Partners' capital account and Credit Realization account.

options.a: partners capital account and credited to realization account
options.b: realization account and credited to capital account
options.c: partners capital account and credited to asset account
options.d: asset account and credited to realization account

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts

question: The objective of a departmental accounts is to ascertain the
solution: The objective of departmental accounts is to determine the performance of each department. It involves keeping separate accounts for every department to assess their profitability and aid decision-making.

options.d: number of staff in the department

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts

question: Which of the following describe trial balance?
solution: A trial balance is a list of all general ledger accounts, showing each account’s name and balance. Each balance is either a debit or a credit.

options.b: It reveals the financial position of a business
options.c: It is a list of accounting balances on the books
options.d: It shows all the entries in the books

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts

question:
In bank reconciliation, when a cash book shows a credit balance, uncredited cheques are:
I. deducted from the cash book balance
II. added to the bank statement balance
III. added to the cash book balance
solution: In bank reconciliation, a credit balance in the cash book means the bank owes the business. Uncredited cheques—those issued but not yet presented to the bank—are deducted from the cash book balance.
subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts

question: Which of the following is a spreadsheet application?
solution: Excel is a spreadsheet application consisting of rows and columns used to organize, sort, and manage data. It allows for performing various arithmetic operations on the data.
subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts

question: Into how many major types can general-purpose computers be classified?
solution:
General-purpose computers are classified into three types:

1. Personal computers (desktops, laptops, smartphones, tablets)

2. Servers, which provide information to other computers on a network

3. Supercomputers, used for complex tasks like weather forecasting and scientific simulations.
subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts

question: Cash receipts and payments involving discounts are entered in
solution: A three-column cash book records cash receipts and payments, including discounts. It has separate columns for discount, cash, and bank transactions, allowing discounts to be recorded alongside cash and bank entries.
subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts

question: Which of the following is used before the appropriation bill is approved?
solution: A provisional general warrant is issued before the appropriation bill is approved. It allows the government to withdraw funds from the consolidated fund to run government operations while the bill is under consideration.
subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts

question: The cost of raw materials consumed is
solution:
Cost of raw materials consumed = Opening stock + Purchases − Closing stock
= 8,000 + 45,000 − 9,500 = ₦43,500.
subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts

question: Prime cost is
solution:
Prime cost = cost of raw materials consumed + direct expenses
= 43,500 + 6450 = #49,950
subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts

question: Factory cost of production
solution:
Factory cost of production = Prime cost + Factory overheads
= 52,950 + (2,800 + 3,200 + 350)
= 52,950 + 6,350 = 56,300.
subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts

question: Production cost is
solution:
production cost = factory cost of production + opening W.I.P - Closing W.I.P
cost = 56,300 +3,250 -3,750 = #55,800
subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts

question:
Taiwo is a sole trader who keeps his petty cash on the imprest system, the imprest amount being #4,000. 

The following transactions took place for a particular month:

Dec 1 petty cash in hand 517
1 petty cash to imprest 3,483

27 Bought postage stamps 210

Amount to be posted to the personal ledger is

question: The class of share to which payment of dividend depends on profit is
solution: Ordinary shares carry dividends that depend on the company’s profits and are not fixed. Forfeited shares are shares taken back by the company due to non-payment of calls. Bonus shares are free additional shares issued to existing shareholders. Preference shares carry a fixed dividend, which is paid before dividends on ordinary shares.
subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts

question: Which of the following is not a credit item in the Sales Ledger Control Account?
solution:
In the Sales Ledger Control Account, credit items include cash received, discount allowed, and returns inward, as they reduce the amount owed by debtors.

A dishonoured cheque increases the debtor’s balance and is therefore debited, not credited. Hence, it is not a credit item.
subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts

question: Which of the following is determined in the Trading Account?
solution: The Trading Account is prepared to determine gross profit or gross loss, and in doing so it calculates the cost of goods sold (COGS).
subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts

question: An example of a self-balancing account is the
solution: A self-balancing account is one whose balance agrees with the total of the related subsidiary ledger. Control accounts (like Sales Ledger Control Account or Purchases Ledger Control Account) are designed for this purpose.
subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts

question: Which of the following transactions is a revenue expenditure?
solution:
Revenue expenditure is money spent on the day-to-day running of a business. Its benefit lasts for less than one year, and it is charged in the same accounting year.

An example is the purchase of fuel for a vehicle, since it is a recurring expense with a short-term benefit.
subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts

question: Suspense account is used in the correction of
solution: A suspense account is a temporary account used to record the difference when the trial balance does not balance. The difference is posted to the suspense account until the errors are found and corrected. It is used for correcting errors that affect the agreement of the trial balance.

options.a: errors that affect the agreement of a trial balance
options.b: errors that affect the accuracy of the net profit
options.c: errors that do not affect the agreement of a trial balance
options.d: all errors in the trial balance

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts

question: When shares are sold at less than the nominal value, it means they are issued at
solution: When shares are sold for less than their nominal (par) value, they are said to be issued at a discount. The nominal value is the face value stated in the company’s constitution. This typically occurs when a company requires immediate funds or struggles to attract investors.
subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts

question: Which of the following is not credited to debtors control account?
solution: Provision for bad debts is not credited to debtors control account. It is not an item of debtors control account. It is always ignored when it is given.
subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts

question: The capital of a sole trader changes as a result of
solution: The capital of a sole trader changes when the owner makes drawings by cheque. Drawings are withdrawals for personal use, and they reduce the owner’s capital in the business.
subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts

question: The balance on the Sales Ledger Control Account at the end of the accounting year represents total
solution: The balance on the Sales Ledger Control Account at the end of the accounting year represents the total trade debtors. It shows the total amount owed to the business by its customers.

options.b: trade debtors at the end of the year
options.d: amount transferred from the Sales Day Book

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts

question: Which of the following is not a source document?
solution: A bank note is not a source document. Source documents provide evidence of business transactions, such as invoices, receipts, credit notes, and debit notes. A bank note is only a means of payment and does not record a specific transaction.
subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts

question: Provision for depreciation on delivery van is charged to
solution: Provision for depreciation on a delivery van is charged to the Profit and Loss Account. Depreciation is the reduction in value of an asset due to wear and tear or obsolescence, and it is treated as an expense when calculating net profit or loss.
subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts

question: A sales daybook is used to record
solution:
A sales day book (or sales journal) records only credit sales of stock.
Cash sales are recorded in the cash book, and sales of fixed assets are recorded in the general journal.

options.b: cash and credit sales of stock

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts

question: Capital expenditure is the
solution:
Capital expenditure is money spent on buying or improving fixed assets like land, buildings, and equipment. It includes costs to acquire new assets or extend the life of existing ones, such as repairing a building’s roof.

So, it means money spent on acquiring fixed assets.

options.a: extra capital paid in by the proprietor
options.b: money spend on buying goods for resale
options.c: money spend on acquiring fixed assets

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts

question: Which of the following is not a real account?
solution: A real account relates to assets and liabilities (except personal accounts) and is carried forward each year. Examples include Building account and Motor Vehicle account. In contrast, a nominal account records income, expenses, losses, or gains, like the Patent account.
subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts

question: In preparing a profit and loss account, a decrease in provision for doubtful debts accounts is treated as
solution: In the profit and loss account, a decrease in provision for doubtful debts is treated as income. This happens because it means fewer bad debts are expected than previously estimated, so the difference is recorded as income.
subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts

question: Which of the following is not a source document?
solution:
A source document is the original proof of a financial transaction, like sales invoices, debit notes, and credit notes.
A journal proper is not a source document; it is a book where unusual or miscellaneous credit transactions are recorded.
subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts

question: The balance sheet as at 31st December, 2014, will show
solution: The balance sheet as at 31st December, 2014, will show the amount of rent that has been prepaid and is yet to be used. This is the rent prepaid at the end of the year, which is ₦400
subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts

question: A financial plan of action expressed in monetary terms is known as
solution: A budget is a financial plan expressed in money or other units (like products or labor hours) for a specific period, usually one year, made in advance to guide business activities.
subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts

question: When the going concern concept is no longer applicable, the fixed assets are recorded at their
solution: The going concern concept assumes a business will keep operating indefinitely. If the business is closing, assets are recorded at their realizable value—the amount they can be sold for—not their original cost.
subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts

question:
Use the following information to answer this question

Rent for 2014 chargeable to the profit and loss account is
solution: The rent for 2014 chargeable to the profit and loss account is calculated by adding the rent paid during the year to the rent prepaid at the beginning of the year and then subtracting the rent prepaid at the end of the year. This gives us ₦3,200 + ₦600 - ₦400 = ₦3,400.
subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts

question: The process of distributing shares to successful applicants is
solution:
Allotment is the process of distributing shares to successful applicants.

Allocation means assigning resources for specific purposes.

Apportionment means dividing something proportionally.

Application is the formal request for shares.
subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts

question: One of the components of factory overhead is
solution:
Factory overheads are indirect manufacturing costs like depreciation of plant and machinery, utilities, rent, and indirect labor.

Raw materials and manufacturing wages are direct costs, while carriage inwards is a cost of transporting raw materials, not overhead.
subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts

question: Which of the following is a primary source of entry into the debtors ledger?
subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts

question: Resources owned and controlled by a business are classified as
solution: Assets are resources owned and controlled by a business that have value. They can be tangible (physical things like buildings and equipment) or intangible (non-physical things like trademarks and patents).
subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts

question: Which of the following is used before the appropriation bill is approved?
solution: A provisional general warrant allows the government to withdraw money from the consolidated fund to run government activities before the appropriation bill is approved.
subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
